region_of_origin: China (Taiwanese-style dim sum, served in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed bun class (FOODON_03302050); no specific yam/taro-filled steamed bun entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers steamed buns (baozi) as a category. No specific yam-filled variant code exists; closest parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for steamed yam buns. SNOMED CT does not have a specific concept for this food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 3 steamed yam buns, each approximately 45 g, totalling approximately 135 g, as served at Din Tai Fung Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed Bun with Taro Filling — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; supplemented with FAO/INFOODS SEA taro paste and steamed wheat bun reference values
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for steamed yam/taro-filled buns exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for plain steamed wheat buns (approximately 60% of weight) with USDA and FAO/INFOODS taro paste filling data (approximately 40% of weight). The filling is mashed taro with sugar and fat. Energy, macros, fibre, sodium, and sugar are derived from this weighted composite. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Steamed wheat bun (baozi) glycemic index — analogous estimate based on steamed white bread / steamed wheat products; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for steamed yam-filled buns. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on steamed wheat buns (baozi, GI ~65 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The taro filling has a moderate GI (~55 per University of Sydney GI Database for taro), and the wheat bun skin dominates the glycemic response. A blended estimate of 65 (medium) is applied. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 60.1) / 100 ≈ 39.1, rounded to 38.9 using 44.5 g carb per 100g × 1.35 serving = 60.1 g carb per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~185 mg per 100 g; ~250 mg per 3-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 buns per sitting, as the sodium content from the seasoned dough and filling can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets of under 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~65) and high carbohydrate content (~44.5 g per 100 g) with added sugar in the taro filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat steamed yam buns as an occasional item, limiting to 1–2 pieces per meal and pairing with protein or fibre-rich foods to blunt the postprandial glycemic response, given the medium GI and significant carbohydrate load per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g) and negligible cholesterol; low trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed yam buns present a relatively low cardiovascular risk per serving due to their steaming preparation method and low cholesterol content, though individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of cumulative saturated fat intake if consuming multiple servings.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Yam Buns (芋泥包, Yù Ní Bāo) are a classic Chinese dim sum item consisting of a soft, pillowy steamed wheat-flour bun skin encasing a smooth, sweetened taro (yam) paste filling. The bun skin is made from wheat flour, yeast, sugar, and shortening, and is hand-pleated into the signature 18-fold pattern associated with Din Tai Fung's craftsmanship. The taro filling is prepared from mashed premium Thai taro blended with sugar and fat to create a dense, mildly sweet, and subtly earthy purple-hued paste. The texture of the bun is light and slightly chewy on the outside, giving way to a creamy, smooth filling. Steamed yam buns are served as part of a dim sum spread or as a dessert course. Nutritionally, each 3-piece serving (~135 g) provides approximately 335 kcal, with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient (~60 g per serving) and moderate fat (~8 g). The medium glycemic index (~65) and added sugar in the filling make portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or blood glucose levels. Sodium content is moderate (~250 mg per serving), relevant for hypertension management. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan due to potential use of lard or dairy-derived shortening. It contains gluten and is not suitable for those with coeliac disease or wheat allergy.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for steamed yam-filled buns (Din Tai Fung style) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy steamed wheat bun data and USDA/FAO taro paste data. GI is an analogous estimate based on steamed wheat bun GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a composite of USDA SR Legacy steamed wheat bun (plain, ~60% weight fraction) and USDA/FAO taro paste filling (~40% weight fraction). No exact Din Tai Fung product nutritional data is publicly available from an approved source. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy steamed bun reference; no hydrogenated fats reported in standard steamed bun formulations. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy steamed bun reference; no animal-derived cholesterol-bearing ingredients in standard formulation (lard variant would add trace cholesterol, but standard formulation uses vegetable shortening). 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based grain and vegetable product with no animal-derived B12 sources. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the composite source references used. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS references for steamed buns or taro. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary composite source references. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source references. 9. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for Din Tai Fung steamed yam buns has not been published. 10. Halal status is uncertain — Din Tai Fung Singapore is not certified halal; is_halal set to false. 11. Serving size of 135 g (3 × 45 g buns) is an estimate based on typical dim sum bun weights; actual Din Tai Fung portion weight may vary. 12. Tamil local name uses a descriptive approximation (steamed soft bread/bun with root vegetable) as no single canonical Tamil term for yam buns exists in standard references. 13. Hindi local name uses a descriptive approximation (steamed arbi buns) as no single canonical Hindi term exists.
